Super Smash Bros. Ultimate fails as a coherent fighting game system. While its surface is polished and content-rich, the underlying mechanics are erratic and inconsistent. Inputs register unpredictably, hitboxes are poorly defined, and movement physics feel imprecise and floaty. The result is a game where causality between player action and on-screen outcome is constantly obscured. The AI routinely reads player inputs, eliminating the possibility of fair pattern learning. Competitive play exists only after stripping the game of most of its own systems — items, random stage hazards, and balance-breaking characters. That alone indicates fundamental design incoherence. The Adventure Mode amplifies the issue: rather than providing structured progression, it relies on artificial grinding, random buffs, and cryptic mechanics to simulate depth. It extends playtime but undermines learning. Smash Ultimate rewards reaction and chaos over intentional mastery. Despite its audiovisual polish and iconic roster, its gameplay lacks the reproducibility and mechanical clarity that define serious fighting games.

Animal Crossing: New Horizons is one of the best games I’ve ever played. It’s such a chill and relaxing experience that you can sink hours into without even realizing it. The freedom you get to design your island, customize your house, and create your own little paradise is unmatched. Whether you’re planting flowers, building bridges, or just hanging out with the adorable villagers, it’s the kind of game that makes you feel at peace. The seasonal events and special updates keep it fresh and exciting, making you want to come back and see what’s **** music and sound effects are so soothing and really add to the vibe of the game. And don’t even get me started on the villagers—they have so much personality and make the game feel alive. You get attached to them, and when they leave, it’s like saying goodbye to a friend. And then there’s Tom Nook, the capitalistic king of the island who somehow manages to make you feel like you’re the one in charge while he’s pulling all the strings behind the scenes. The man’s a genius when it comes to selling you overpriced homes and upgrades, but you can’t help but love him for it. It’s hilarious how he’s both a business mogul and your best buddy at the same time.I used to play it all the time, but sadly, I sold my Switch because I wasn’t using it much. Now, I really regret it. I miss spending time in my little island paradise, creating new designs, and seeing what my favorite villagers are up to. It’s such a great game that I’d love to pick up again. It’s just pure comfort and joy, and the kind of game that makes you smile every time you play.

Like many other Pokémon fans, I really wanted to like this game, but it fell flat in so many disappointing ways. I think they had some great ideas, but somehow almost every part of the game just felt unfinished. For what is supposed to be a AAA title from one of the biggest gaming studios, this was not acceptable for the price they demanded.I liked the switch-up of the battle system, but I think they needed to push it further. Even taking the cooldowns into consideration, it still doesn’t give much reason to pick any movesets you wouldn’t have picked in the other games. You’re still just choosing moves that deal high damage and have type advantage. I wish there were more combo potential, but every move feels sluggish to use because of the weird delay between calling out a move and the Pokémon actually performing it, along with whatever wind-up animation is required. I also feel that trying to access the item menu during battle feels clunky. Maybe a little more user feedback when you try to open the menu and can’t—idk, it just needs improvement.I liked the creative approach of making an open-world game set inside a big city, but what an absolutely boring and repetitive city this is. Everything looks the same and feels too pristine, in a doctor’s-office kind of way. Textures are repeated everywhere, and if you don’t have a Switch 2, good luck dealing with the pixelation in the ground tiles throughout the entire city.I liked the idea of jumping puzzles, but again, these felt rushed. Why couldn’t our character just be able to jump? I really don’t care for the lack of mobility in an “open-world” setting.Lastly, and what really drove me to write this review, is the stupidly horrible storytelling and writing. I get that this is intended for kids, but give them at least a little more credit. The premise starts off on an unrealistic note and just keeps spiraling downward. I will give the game credit for giving all the characters personality, but it ultimately went to waste. I was here for the Z-to-A grind, but somehow they made even that awkward after a certain point (I won’t spoil anything). Near the end of the game, I was just spamming the A button, trying to get these cringey scenes over with so I could be **** yeah, I wanted this to be a great game—I really did—but Game Freak and Nintendo dropped the ball MEGA hard on this one.

New Leaf lets you jump into the fun immediately, but here you have to wait soo long until the interesting part. I actually didn't get to the terraforming because I put the game down a year ago and haven't checked on it since. I've never lost interest in a game so quickly. Another thing that bothers me is how washed down and boring the villagers (or islanders) are now. I know there were people who didn't like how some villagers were mean or sassy in older games, but you can't deny that it added spice and made them feel like real neighbors (because not everybody will instantly love you upon first meeting in real life, obviously). But now all the villagers have the same cookie-cutter niceness, making them lose what made them distict. Even villagers with snooty and grouchy personality types lack the edge they used to have. New Horizons was only critically acclaimed because it released during the pandemic when everyone was dying of boredom. If it was released before or a year after the pandemic, I'd imagine people would've been much more critical of it. I think they tried too hard to be "cozy" and "chill" that it backfired and made the game boring and bland.

Despite it not being worth $80, Mario Kart World is genuinely a fun game! There are several cool things this game has to offer such as rail grinding, wall riding, Knockout Tour, the NPC characters such as Conkdor and Cataquack and also Photo Mode. The rail grinding/wall riding are really good incase if you wanna practice shortcuts. An elimination-based mode in Mario Kart is a genius idea. The NPC characters are weird, but at the same time, awesome additions just like DK Jr., R.O.B. and Link and Inkling were in Super, DS and 8 Deluxe respectively.  The intermissions are boring, but are slowly becoming more promising after version 1.4.0 came out, specifically the intermissions that go from/to Koopa Troopa Beach. Whether it’d be changing the layout and adding more obstacles or having the race on the track go from one laps to two. Free Roam is quite dope and I kinda find it funny how people call it "empty" when there are NPC Yoshis, Toads and Shy Guys and moving vehicles that you can see wherever you drive around. As much as I love Free Roam, I think there should be a way to make it a bit more enjoyable. For example, let’s say you find a P-Switch near Toad’s Factory, King Bob-omb suddenly appears and takes over the factory and the main objective is to defeat him Mario Kart DS-style. Once you do that, you don’t get a sticker like usual, instead you unlock King Bob-omb and he gets automatically added into your roster, a bit similar to how you unlock NPC drivers when you get transformed by Kamek (which is now easier thanks to the Custom Items, even though I unlocked all the NPCs before the Custom Items and the UFO update.). Speaking of stickers, it gets quite annoying every time you get one once you earn an achievement. While some of the stickers look cool, it serves no purpose and it just gets placed onto your kart. This doesn’t affect me since I don’t have NSO, but a lot of players (myself included) didn’t like the change made to the controversial version 1.1.2 update where Nintendo made it so that anytime you select a random track in Online, you have a chance of racing on an intermission. Although this wasn’t a good change, I find it really unnecessary and dramatic that people on social media (especially YouTube) were calling the game "dead" even when people were still playing Online after the update. I absolutely dislike Battle Mode though, the arena selection consists of race tracks while two of the arenas are actual ones, but they are both retro. Three of the five modes from 8 Deluxe were cut (I hope they come back as DLC though), it’s just Balloon Battle and Coin Runners. I played both once and I was like "yeah, I’m done". Base game 8’s battle mode was worse in my opinion since they only had Balloon Battle and all the arenas are just race tracks with the exact same layout. I think we should compare World with base game 8 instead of Post-Booster Course Pass 8 Deluxe since base game 8 had the same flaws World does. One time before World came out, an interviewer asked Yabuki about 200cc and Yabuki stated that 200cc won’t be available at launch, Mario Kart 8 didn’t have 200cc at launch as well. But I miss some things from 8 Deluxe that didn’t make the cut in World, such as 200cc which I just talked about, Diddy Kong and Funky Kong, the Miis, etc. and I hope they come back as DLC. I think DLC could be announced during the next Direct which could happen sometime in February or March (just don’t bring back Peachette or Pink Gold Peach, please. ). If they’re doing a new open-world map with more tracks or more characters, I could see it being paid. If they’re just doing 200cc or new costumes (especially for DK and Pauline), then it could be free. I also heard there’s a chance that a cross-promo for DK Bananza could happen where K. Rool and Void Kong could become playable, which would be really cool.

Overall, Mario Kart World is a very incredible game. I’ve been enjoying the game since Day 1 and I still do to this day. If you’re planning to buy a Switch 2, I recommend you buy this game along with the console.

So yeah, I’ve probably spent way too many hours in Hyrule, but honestly, Breath of the Wild is just one of those games that you can’t ever really stop thinking about once you’ve experienced it. It’s not just a game, it’s like this weird, beautiful, lonely, and yet totally chaotic world that just lets you be whoever you want to be. I remember the first time I stepped out of the Shrine of Resurrection—that pan over the Great Plateau? Absolute cinema. It’s a masterclass in game design because it doesn’t give you a checklist or a map full of annoying icons. It just says, "Hey, see that mountain? You can climb it. See that weird glow in the distance? Go figure it out." And you do. You just wander. You get distracted by a butterfly, then a group of Bokoblins, then you find a shrine, and suddenly four hours have passed and you haven’t even touched the main quest. That kind of freedom is rare. Most games talk about being open world, but then they hit you with invisible walls or level-gating. BotW doesn't care about any of that. If you’re smart enough (or crazy enough), you can go straight to the final boss from the start. That’s just insane to me.I love **** let’s talk about the gear for a sec because, man, the items in this game are actually legendary once you figure out how to use them. I’m a huge horse guy in games, and the way they handled the horses here feels so grounded. But the real game-changer is when you get your hands on the Ancient Saddle and Bridle. Before those, your horse was kinda just... there. You’d leave it behind because you climbed a cliff and it couldn't follow you. But with that Ancient set? You whistle and boom, teleportation. It makes the bond with your horse feel so much more useful and, honestly, it adds to that cinematic feeling when you’re galloping through a field at night and you can just summon your ride whenever you need to make a quick getaway from a Guardian. It’s those little things, the quality of life stuff mixed with the "Chemistry Engine" where fire actually burns things and lightning is attracted to your metal shield, that makes the world feel like a real place rather than just a digital **** thing that really got to me, and I know I’m a total **** for this kind of stuff, is the whole tragic, romantic undertone of the story, especially involving Mipha. I’m a big fan of those cinematic, slightly heartbreaking backstories, and hers just hits differently. It’s not forced down your throat, it’s just there in the memories and the way the Zora people talk about her. The fact that she made that Zora Armor specifically for Link as a proposal... and then she died before she could even give it to him or tell him how she felt? That’s some heavy stuff. Every time I see her spirit on Vah Ruta, or use Mipha’s Grace to save my skin in a fight, it feels like this lingering, protective love that survived a hundred years of darkness. It gives the whole "save the world" thing a lot more weight because it’s not just about a kingdom, it’s about the people who loved you and aren't there anymore. It’s subtle, it’s romantic in a very sad way, and it makes the quiet moments of the game feel so much **** music, man... it's just perfect. Some people wanted a big, bombastic theme playing 24/7, but they’re wrong. The silence is what makes the music work. You're wandering through the ruins of a village, and you just hear these sparse, lonely piano notes that feel like they're mourning the world with you. But then, when things get real, the soundtrack absolutely **** final push toward Hyrule Castle is probably the most "epic" feeling I’ve ever had in a Zelda game. The way the music shifts from the outdoor theme to that heavy, orchestral march as you get closer to the sanctum is incredible. And that final fight with Calamity Ganon? It’s pure spectacle. When the Divine Beasts fire their lasers and the Champions' spirits are there with you, it feels like this massive culmination of everything you’ve suffered through. The second phase out on the field, with the sun low on the horizon and Zelda calling out to you while the music swells... that’s the kind of stuff I play games for. It’s grand, it’s emotional, and it feels like a true ending to a century-old tragedy. You’re not just hitting a boss; you’re ending a nightmare. It’s the perfect mix of gameplay and pure cinematic storytelling.Seriously, if you haven't played this yet, I don't know what to tell you. It’s a masterpiece. The weapons break, the rain makes you slide off cliffs, and you’ll probably die a hundred times to lightning or falling rocks, but that’s the charm. It’s a raw, beautiful experience that respects the player’s intelligence and emotions.I still go back just to ride my horse through the fields sometimes. It's just that good.

Pokémon Legends: Z-A is a game that I genuinely enjoy playing. I like this game, and I keep coming back to it. It was clear that Pokémon was going to release another Legends-style title, and when you do that, you are expected to improve in many areas, introduce new mechanics, and still keep the core identity of the franchise while expanding the story, multiplayer elements, and extra content.Legends: Z-A launched on October 16, 2025, but despite the ambition behind it, this does not really feel like a Pokémon game. Battles are no longer turn-based, and the map is noticeably smaller. I believe the biggest mistake Pokémon made was trying to completely change the combat system for a community that was used to classic, turn-based battles enhanced by new mechanics. Once again, the same problem appears: this does not feel like Pokémon.Without turn-based combat, the game promotes a faster and far less strategic playstyle. You stop thinking about lowering stats, setting up strategies, or carefully choosing your moves. Instead, battles often turn into attack spamming, where being faster matters more than making smart decisions. The game technically gives you tools to play strategically, but the combat system itself does not encourage that approach at all.This leads to another major problem: the pacing of battles. The game is extremely fast, and most fights feel the same. The majority of Pokémon can be defeated in just a few hits. If you stop to think too much about which move to use, your Pokémon will already be knocked out. Status effects like paralysis or sleep feel almost useless, as battles end too quickly for them to **** other major type of battle is against the rampaging Mega Evolutions. These fights last longer and are more challenging, with Pokémon attacking the player directly. However, even if you successfully dodge an attack, your Pokémon will often still get hit because it simply follows you instead of dodging on its own. In Legends: Z-A, getting distracted for just a second can cost you the entire fight.Another issue that hurts strategy is the lack of basic information during battles. You cannot see the type of the Pokémon you are facing unless you already know it or completely stop the action to check the Pokédex. This would have been an easy quality-of-life feature to include, but it is missing.I also dislike how important battles feel rushed and less exciting. Even though the game tries to make combat feel fresher, it ends up losing the tension and excitement that Pokémon battles usually have.Visually, the game repeats mistakes from previous entries. Buildings look flat and lifeless, almost like a model or a diorama, which clashes heavily with their interiors that are far more detailed and visually appealing. Main characters look great, but NPCs have a very poor visual style. They do not look like they belong in the same game as the main cast, and many of them look nearly identical. This mix of styles makes the world feel unnatural and sometimes even **** of the main mechanics of the game is the Z-A ranking system, where you start at rank Z and work your way up to rank A through nightly battles that reward points and eventually unlock promotion matches. The idea itself is excellent. However, when you reach rank U, the game suddenly boosts you all the way up to rank F with a weak narrative justification that completely breaks the pacing of both the story and character progression. Since this is the main mechanic of the game and even referenced in the title, it could have been handled much better. Progression could have depended on performance, or players could have started at a higher rank if they performed well early on, but instead the game chooses an abrupt and unnatural shortcut.Exploration is another weak point. Everything in Legends: Z-A takes place in a small city, but it is not truly open. Even when you explore rooftops, everything looks the same and offers nothing beyond generic items. Wild areas are very small, and when fighting wild Pokémon, groups often attack both the player and the Pokémon at the same time. Since you can only send out one Pokémon, the best option is often to run away. There are no distinctive biomes or meaningful differences between wild areas, and important places are mostly shops or Pokémon Centers that all look identical. Side quests are a repetitive filler, completely limited to battling or delivering Pokémon to NPCs.Despite all of these issues, I will continue playing the game. The story is very interesting, and discovering new evolutions and Mega Evolutions is still one of the most exciting parts of the game.

I will not deny that this game has potential. As someone who has constantly played Wii Sports and Wii Sports Resort for the last 15 years, however, this game is a bit of a letdown. There is a lot to love here, though. All the sports have engaging gameplay, especially Bowling and Golf. I especially enjoyed the "twists" put on each game online. However, there is no practical way to progress (besides ranking up). In the original Wii Sports, as your skill rank increased, the opponents you faced became more challenging. However, Switch Sports does not work the same. There are only a few set levels of difficulty, significantly reducing the challenge and replayability of this game. Locking many cosmetics behind an online paywall does not help, either. There are certainly bright spots to this game, but ultimately it is just another example of nostalgia bait that - while the intentions were good, was just another franchise revival.

This is gonna be controversial, but Super Mario Party is in my opinion the best Mario Party. I've reviewed all the other parties, but this one stands out to me. Although I'm a bit sad only 4 boards appear, UNLIKE Mario Party Superstars, each board here is filled with fun and competitiveness. Also, they replaced the great 1-2-3, 4-5-6, 1-10 and Slow dice blocks with the even better character specific dice.
Instead of blindlessly picking Yoshi every game, you think about the board layout and whether your character's dice will help you on the way. Each dice block is perfectly balanced (except for Daisy's and Donkey Kong's) and provides for a strategic take that refines the gameplay. Plus, though many shrugged them off as 'meh they're fine', the minigames are FANTASTIC, and even better than Mario Parties 4, 5, 9, 10 and Superstar's excellent ones (My favourite being Fuzzy Flight School). However if you're not looking to play the boards over and over and want to face some other new challenges, Mario opens his gloved palm and offers you a buncha more fun side modes, but I won't spoil it for you. If you like Mario Party, or you want to be the attractive guy who pulls out a Switch OLED, Joy-Cons and a copy of this game from your pocket, this is a must play.

The fourth entry in the beloved Paper Mario series, Sticker Star isn't what many wanted out of the series, being unafraid to break the tried and true traditions of its predecessors. But the result turned out quite brilliantly, in my opinion. Stickers provide a new and engaging way to battle, with nearly 100 different types to chain together and experiment with, and some of the puzzles will certainly give you a run for your money (though some of them are perhaps a little *too* tough). The Mushroom Kingdom may perhaps be a little too familiar a setting for longtime fans, particularly to those that enjoyed the more unique settings of previous Paper Mario entries, but make no mistake--its realms are just as vibrant, colorful, and papery as ever, made even more appealing by the addition of 3D visuals. And perhaps most admirably, the game is continually inventive and always finds new ways to impress throughout the lengthy 30+ hour campaign, amid many witty bits of dialogue that'll ensure you're in for a good laugh.

It's inevitable that a game that bucks so many traditions will be controversial, especially as a successor to a classic like The Thousand-Year Door. But despite this, Sticker Star gets it right where it matters, and that's in providing you 30 hours of a joyful and colorful adventure--and one you can take with you, too. It has proven difficult for many fans of the Nintendo 64 and GameCube classics to accept Sticker Star's rather unconventional style, but you've truly never played anything like this before and it's absolutely worth a look. As of the time of writing, the game tends to fetch around $20 on eBay, so you certainly don't have to look far, either.

Pokemon Legends ZA dissapoints in many ways, however in my opinion dosent warrant the absolute slander of being renowned as one of the worst games ever made. Starting with the pros, ZAs combat system and gameplay loop are very well refined and polished, where the game can be genuinely addicting to play at times, transitioning from participating in the ZA Royale, taking on the Rogue Mega Evolution Pokemon, and exploring the Wild Zones. The combat system feels refreshing and refined, which is not an easy feat due to this being Pokemons first time fully shifting to real time combat in the main series. As for the many negatives, these can be summarised down to the poorly paced main campaign, sloppy writing, lack of voice acting, lack of detailing in the already small Lumiose City, and the very bare bones and unengaging traversal and platforming. This game feels very behind its time in its narrative presentation, with bloated segments of dialogue with nothing of actual substance. The games narrative can be very tone deaf, and is horribly paced with literally all of this games story being shown in the last 2 hours of the game. The world is not excusable for a 2025 release either, with a clear lack of effort when it comes to the detailing and making Lumiose City feel alive. The game is also a further downgrade compared to Legends Arceus in the traversal department, where Legends ZA only offers you a basic sprint and a not very intuitive glide for vertical movement. The platforming segments are dull and feel like theyre there to purely pad timing, due to the lack of traversal mechanisms. My 6/10 dosent factor in the DLC, as I am frankly not interested in purchasing it at all. The reception has been abysmall, with everyone saying its way worse then even the mediocre base game. I would not recommend paying for this game at full price at all, however it can be a fun enough playthrough if bought second hand.

I kinda went into this game knowing about things like it's performance issues, and to be honest, that's my only major downside with this (aside from the lackluster story, but that's an issue with most modern Pokemon Games, so I stopped caring about the story as much and started expecting lackluster stories). This game is genuinely the most fun I've ever had with a Pokemon game, and it's all because of the open world aspect of it. It feels so free, it feels like a real adventure, and the fact that there are rarely any hard roadblocks keeping you out of specific areas (as in unnatural ones, like a random herd of Psyduck blocking the way to a city) makes it feel that much more free. This also kind of helps with the issue of difficulty, but not really. Difficulty almost kept this from getting an 8, but I found that the lack of difficulty was kind of balanced out by how fun it was, so it barely made it. I didn't find a lot of unique strategies used by trainers, because the closest thing to a strategy someone would have is something to counter 1 super-effective type, which is more than some games have, but still... would've liked to have seen more use of held items. And I can't ignore the performance issues. The game can go below 20 fps sometimes, but mostly, it handles 30 to 40FPS well... that is, unless you go in a more crowded area. The crowded areas are so laggy, and I think it's a byproduct of the graphics, which are gorgeous, but gorgeous in the way a poisonous plant can be; yeah, it's pretty to look at... but once it does stuff, it ain't gonna be pretty, that's for sure. Sure, you can just play it on a stronger platform, but I don't know if Nintendo's next console will be backward compatible with the Switch, and if it is, I don't know how much more bearable it can make the lag, but still. I would've given this a 9... but the lackluster story already barred it from a 10, and the performance issues were major enough to knock it down to an 8. I also don't wanna count DLC into this review, but if I did, then this would be a 9. The DLC is so good, but we're talking about the main game. It's the most fun Pokemon game so far, but it's also the worst performing (as in how it runs), and probably the only game where I've ever wanted the graphics to be worse so that I don't have to panic about the game crashing whenever I enter an area with 8 or more breathing organisms. Performance issues aside, this game's fun. I would recommend if you can either handle lag, or if you know of a way to run the game on something stronger.

This game is bad, but not as absolutely awful as some have suggested.
The main bad thing about it is that it ditches some of the basic elements of an RPG, while still pretending to be one. I actually quite liked the gimmick of using stickers in battle, and having a certain amount of consumable attacks. However, the sticker interface is clunky and not user-friendly; it can take ages to find the stickers you want to use and sometimes you might find you don't even have the ones you've been looking for for ages. A menu would have been better. Another concern about battles is the fact that you no longer gain EXP at the end of a battle, which means that there is absolutely no incentive to play battles at all unless they are unavoidable. The bosses are quite fun but very easy with the right stickers available and sometimes impossible without them.
Also, the areas are more bland, and lack the original characters that made the first 2 PM games so great, in fact every character is called 'A Toad'. It really wouldn't have been hard to at least give them names or different colored spots, or have female toads. The fact that you are going through a linear level instead of looking around areas to complete quests makes it boring as well.
I mean, it's still an entertaining game. There are small amounts of humor and a few more interesting areas, including a roller coaster queue and a quiz show. The soundtrack is average, and the graphics look good in 3D.
You can find fun in this game. But if you want a game similar to the first 2 Paper Mario games, prepare to be extremely disappointed.

Rating: 5.9

This game is fine for offline play. HOWEVER.. the online experience is excruciating.

Since the game is designed with very specific moves that are effective to continually spam, most players are not interested in playing against you; they are interested in abusing you. Because in Ultimate, you don't have to be able to play well to reach a high level, you just need to know how to spam and tire people. Because of that, most players you encounter that use the same character will play in almost the exact same way. They abuse the game's main inherent flaw, then rinse and repeat. Again and again. Some characters are better balanced, of course, but at least HALF of the roster is DESIGNED to have a gameplay equivalent to: run away and spam. I can't fathom this decision, which results in very boring, predictable, and infuriating gameplay.

Also, some characters have a move-set so prioritary over all the other characters that it's virtually impossible to gain the upper hand in what would usually be a close match-up.

I cannot insist enough: the game is designed to favor spamming and "camping", two of the most obnoxious things people abuse in online gaming (aside from teabagging, which also occurs all the time... people are just that arrogant..).

Then, prepare to play in majority against DLC characters. Is it because they're cool? No, it's because of the pay to win element. DLC characters are faster and stronger than the base roster, and make it even more easier to cheese your opponents to victory. There's no question about it.

Also to note, if you don't want to rematch someone, don't worry, the game will most likely rematch you against them anyway. It just doesn't care. Then you have to endure another session with them (you can't disconnect or SD on purpose, otherwise the game locks you out of the online mode).

Finally, some of the levels are so visually intense that the fighting is hard to follow, and some music choices are more than questionable, so you have to spend 5-6 minutes listening to the most annoying loop of 8bit garbage.

After 800 hours in the game, that's my conclusion. It's an infuriating mess and I can't stand it anymore. I regret getting into it. I thought about smashing a screwdriver in the game cartridge multiple times, but I'll just sell it instead. I pity the **** who will buy it. Do not recommend.

Let's start with the obvious: This game is unpolished. Framerate issues, graphics problems, bugs, you name it. But the issues don't stop there: there is no level scaling and the game quickly becomes to easy.
However, if you ignore the technical issues, go through the different gyms and bosses in ascending order, and try to only use Pokemon of the same level as the respective gyms, this is the best Pokemon game of all time.
The open world is great. The feeling of exploring the world, discovering items, new Pokemon and NPCs is amazing. Annoying random encounters have been removed. And there have been a bunch of quality of life changes that make the game significantly less grindy. The new Terastalizing mechanic makes battles even more interesting.
All in all, the core gameplay loop of catching Pokemon and building teams has never been as fun as in this game, especially if the player takes some steps to ensure that things go in the right order and are not too easy. There is something in the classic linear top-down games that gives them life and coziness that are not present here. But still, this is a very good Pokemon game!

Is Paper Mario: Sticker Star really as bad as people say?

Well in some ways, no. This game has some redeeming qualities that no one seems to talk about, like clever and funny dialogue, extremely good music, and fun boss fights, even though the battle system isn't that good. And that's the main problem with this game. The game revolves around these stickers, which you collect throughout your journey and use for battles. The attacks are really cool, but you can only use them one time then they're gone forever. You almost never have to worry about running out since each level is filled to the brim with them. But despite that, I always avoided fights whenever I could to save stickers for bosses. That's not a good thing.

Like all Paper Mario games, you will have to solve puzzles to progress through the game. While some of the puzzles are brilliant, others are practically impossible without a guide, because they revolve around these "things." "Things" are 3D objects found within the game's 38 levels. Once you collect these "things", you can then go to Decalburg, which is the hub world, to paperize them which will allow you to use them. You can paperize as many "things" as you can, as long as your inventory has the space for them. They can also be used in battles, and some battles require the use of a specific one. But there's no real way to tell which of the 64 "things" you need to use without a guide.

So is this game good? Well if you haven't played a Paper Mario game that was released prior to Paper Mario Sticker Star, you will enjoy this game. But longtime fans of the series will be disappointed that this game is not like its predecessors, with no exp or leveling up, no real reason to battle, and the lack of interesting characters. As for me, the perfect word to describe this game is "ok". It's not bad, but it's far from a magnum opus.

The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild is not just a game, it is a revolution. A true masterpiece that redefines the very idea of the open world, it doesn’t simply invite exploration; it demands it. From the very first moments, the player is driven by pure curiosity, by the irresistible urge to wander, to climb, to look beyond the next hill just to see what lies there. Exploration is never forced or guided,it feels natural, organic, and deeply personal.What truly sets Breath of the Wild apart is its extraordinary artistic direction and attention to detail. Every landscape feels hand-crafted, every ruin tells a silent story, and every sunrise over Hyrule is a moment worth stopping for. The game’s physics system is nothing short of astonishing: fire spreads with the wind, metal conducts electricity, and the environment reacts intelligently to the player’s actions. This level of systemic depth makes experimentation endlessly rewarding and turns even simple encounters into memorable experiences.Most of all, Breath of the Wild is profoundly immersive. It creates a version of Hyrule that feels alive, vast, and breathtakingly beautiful,a world you never want to leave. Hours pass unnoticed as the game constantly fuels your desire to explore just a little more, to uncover one more secret, to lose yourself completely. It is a game that reshapes expectations, sets a new standard for open-world design, and remains unforgettable long after the console is turned off. Simply one of the best game of all time, or perhaps the greatest.

I wanted to enjoy this game, and for a little while (once you get past the extremely long, hand holding tutorial, I'm talking 1-2 hours) it was fun, but it gets old FAST,and once you notice the issues they become glaringly obvious, making you how much you spent, and who you gave your money to if they care so little about quality.

The good:
-Pokemon models are charming and colorful.
-The concept of the world building is interesting,or was to me.
-non turn based combat mixes it up a bit (though I never played Arceus so I'm not sure how it compares to the combat there)

The not so good:
-the city is tiny and extremely repetitive. The buildings function more as blocks walling you in, you can't enter many buildings, and the textures are pasted on (badly in many cases, texture and color not lining up, windows and pillars are flat images like wallpaper on cardboard cut out squares keeping you inside with very little immersion,can't unsee this, hard to not notice.)
- the music. I have always loved pokemon music, but why is the entire city (which is the whole map) one single song wherever you go? Day, night, all the same song on repeat. Why not different sections of the city with different tracks to make it feel more alive?
-all the box's are standing still, or walking a few steps going no where. No one eats, or battles, or does anything, they just standin idle mode. I feel like I'm play a very early access game.

-locking some of the mega evolutions behind paid dlc that was announced before the game even came out feels like you're devaluing the original game without extra dlc, and for what? Even more money? If that content is ready why not put it in the base game. Dlc used to be extra optional content, not pieces of base game sectioned off with its own price tag.

-almost all shops are just display menus, you can't go in.

-parkour and climbing onto rooftops sounds fun as a concept, as a concept this game sounds fun in many ways, but in reality they have let it fall flat. Every roof is just flat sections of copy pasted roof images, and the parkour is just random boxes and ladders everywhere. You climb or roll, no jumping, does not feel intuitive.

-you can't pet your pokemon, or play with them, they're just assets, no personality. 

I feel like at this point they're selling the brand and concept of pokemon, the games themselves have been losing heart for years now. Very sad to see.
Don't waste your money, I reccomend waiting for another release, see if they are at least willing to put 3d assets on buildings.
